companyCaseStudy.title: How Zoom Scaled to 300M Daily Meeting Participants: building a realtime video backbone under 30x growth
companyCaseStudy.summary: When the world went remote Zoom jumped from millions to 300 million daily meeting participants almost overnight. This case study shows the architecture and operational choices that let Zoom absorb billions of meeting minutes, keep joins fast, and keep video usable under extreme, bursty load.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook: 30x growth, 300M daily participants, billions of meeting minutes

In early 2020 Zoom moved from being a niche conferencing product to handling an unprecedented global surge. Zoom publicly reported hitting roughly 300 million daily meeting participants and, on peak days, billions of meeting minutes. That abruptly changed requirements: meetings had to connect reliably across NATs, media quality had to survive bad mobile networks, and backend control planes had to orchestrate millions of low-latency connections per minute.

### The problem

- Real-time video is stateful and latency-sensitive: every millisecond and packet loss matters.
- Sudden, sustained growth (dozens of millions → hundreds of millions daily participants) meant static capacity assumptions failed.
- NAT traversal, firewalls and ISP variability required robust relaying (TURN) and media routing.
- Central control plane and signaling needed to coordinate millions of concurrent sessions with sub-second join times.

### The engineering challenges

1. Media scaling: naive full-mesh P2P or unoptimized relays do not scale beyond small meetings.
2. Global routing: minimize round-trip time (RTT) by placing media relays near users, but routing decisions must be fast.
3. Autoscaling under spiky load: ensure headroom for sudden surges without enormous idle costs.
4. Monitoring & incident response: surface quality degradation (packet loss, jitter, join failures) quickly and correlate to infra or network issues.

### Zoom's solution — story arc

1) Hybrid media topology: P2P for tiny 1:1 calls, SFU/Selective Forwarding and optimized relays for group calls

- For 1:1 calls Zoom can often go direct peer-to-peer (NAT permitting), reducing server costs and improving latency.
- For multi-party calls Zoom routes media through a fleet of media relays (a selective-forwarding unit / optimized MCU-like pipeline). The relay handles packet forwarding, stream adaptation and recording hooks so endpoints don't need to upload multiple streams to many peers.

2) Massive global relay footprint (hundreds of POPs) + smart routing

- Zoom expanded its relay Points-of-Presence (PoPs) globally and developed a fast routing decision layer so clients connect to a nearby relay over UDP when possible (TURN fallback over TCP/443 when necessary).
- Routing considers geography, measured latency, and current load on relays.

3) Optimized media pipeline and codec usage

- Hardware-accelerated H.264 encoding paths on mobile and desktop reduced CPU usage per stream and improved battery life.
- Adaptive bitrate and forward-error-correction (FEC) tuned to real-world packet-loss profiles preserved intelligible audio/video even on lossy paths.

4) Autoscaling, reserved capacity and hybrid cloud

- Zoom used a hybrid approach: reserved/contracted capacity across cloud providers and their own infrastructure plus rapid autoscaling. This mix let them guarantee a base of capacity to survive extreme spikes while using autoscaling to absorb tail demand.
- Edge relay instances are lightweight and horizontally scalable; signaling/control planes run in Kubernetes-like clusters with aggressive autoscaling policies.

5) Observability + automated mitigations

- Real-time telemetry (join times, packet loss, jitter, bitrate) is ingested into the control plane and used to trigger routing shifts (move participants to a different PoP), autoscale relays, or adjust codecs.
- Automated playbooks (runbooks) and chaos-style testing ensured the ops teams could respond quickly when an ISP or region had issues.

### Architecture (simplified)

```mermaid
graph LR
  C[Client / App] -->|Signaling Websocket| S[Signaling / Auth]
  C -->|STUN/TURN| NAT[NAT/Firewall]
  NAT --> MR[Media Relay PoP]
  MR --> MR2[Regional Relay Cluster]
  MR2 --> Storage[Recordings & Cloud Storage]
  S --> Control[Control Plane: routing, session mgmt]
  Control --> Telemetry[Observability & Auto-scaler]
  Telemetry --> Ops[Pager / Runbooks]
```

### Before vs After (representative metrics)

| Metric | Before (pre-surge) | After (scaled architecture) |
|--------|--------------------:|---------------------------:|
| Daily meeting participants | ~tens of millions | ~300 million (public milestone) |
| Peak meeting minutes / day | tens/hundreds of millions | billions (multi-billion peak days) |
| Typical join time (median) | ~8s (older stacks) | ~2s (optimizations + closer PoPs) |
| Media relay RTT to client | regional averages higher | many PoPs → lower median RTT |
| Resilience to packet loss | degraded audio/video | adaptive bitrate + FEC preserves calls |

### Results & impact

- Zoom handled a sustained global load it had never designed for and maintained usability in most geographies. Public milestones include ~300M daily meeting participants and multi-billion meeting-minutes peaks.
- The hybrid relay + P2P model kept costs manageable: small meetings stay P2P; large meetings use optimized relays.
- Observability-driven routing reduced regional call failures by enabling fast remediation and automated re-routing.

### Trade-offs and lessons learned

- Reserved capacity vs pure autoscale: reserving base capacity is more expensive but necessary for predictable join latency under flash spikes.
- Hybrid topologies complicate code (P2P + SFU paths) but reduce bandwidth cost and latency overall.
- Global PoPs are effective: routing decisions must be fast and backed by accurate telemetry.

### Practical takeaways for your realtime system

- Use hybrid media paths: P2P where possible, relays for scale and recording. That saves bandwidth and reduces overall cost.
- Invest in global PoPs and fast routing: reducing RTT is one of the biggest improvements to perceived quality.
- Telemetry drives automation: instrument join latency, packet loss, and jitter, and use that data to migrate users automatically.
keyTakeaways: Hybrid topology (P2P for small calls, relays/SFU for groups) reduces cost and improves latency at scale.
Global relay PoPs + smart routing shrunk join times and median RTT—critical when daily participants hit ~300M.
Combine reserved capacity with autoscaling and real-time telemetry to survive flash surges and keep UX acceptable.
relatedTopics: Autoscaling and capacity planning for bursty workloads

concept.title: Agreeing in the Wild: Raft (Consensus Made Practical)
concept.summary: Distributed systems need a reliable way to agree on state despite failures. Raft is a leader-based consensus algorithm that makes safety and simplicity practical for replicated logs, config changes, and distributed coordination.
concept.explanation:
## Why it matters

Imagine a team of secretaries who must keep identical copies of a ledger. If one secretary disappears or hands in the wrong page, how do the rest stay consistent? In distributed systems, replicated state (leader election, writes, metadata) must remain consistent despite crashes, network hiccups, and delays. Consensus algorithms solve *agreeing on the next entry* problem — Raft does this with a focus on *understandability* and operational practicality.

**Analogy:** *Raft is like electing a meeting chair.* The chair proposes decisions (log entries), attendees listen and acknowledge, and once a majority agree the decision is considered committed. If the chair disappears, a new chair is elected, but the group never accepts conflicting decisions.

---

## How Raft works (high level)

1. **Leader election**: Nodes start as followers. If a follower doesn't hear heartbeat, it starts an election (becomes a candidate) and asks others to vote. A majority wins leadership for a term.
2. **Log replication**: Clients send writes to the leader. The leader appends to its log and sends AppendEntries RPCs to followers. Once a majority persist, the leader commits and informs followers to apply the entry.
3. **Safety**: A new leader must have all committed entries; logs with higher terms are preferred. This prevents committed entries from being lost.
4. **Log compaction**: Snapshots compress old log entries to keep storage bounded.

### Sequence of a write (visual)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Client->>Leader: Write(key, value)
  Leader->>Follower1: AppendEntries(term, prevIndex, entry)
  Leader->>Follower2: AppendEntries(term, prevIndex, entry)
  Follower1-->>Leader: Ack
  Follower2-->>Leader: Ack
  Leader->>Client: Success (committed)
  Leader->>Follower1: CommitIndex update
  Leader->>Follower2: CommitIndex update
```

### Before / After: Without vs With Raft

| Aspect | Without Raft (ad-hoc replication) | With Raft (leader-based consensus) |
|--------|-----------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Agreement on order | Race conditions, divergent logs | Single leader orders entries, enforced majority commit |
| Fault handling | Manual, error-prone | Automatic leader election and failover |
| Implementation complexity | High (edge cases everywhere) | Predictable protocol with clear states |
| Recovery of leader loss | Inconsistent or lost writes | New leader ensures committed writes persist |

---

## Core concepts (brief)

- **Term**: logical clock, increases on each election; used to pick freshest leader.
- **Leader / Follower / Candidate**: node states. Followers respond to leader; candidates request votes.
- **Majority quorum**: progress requires agreement from >50% of nodes.
- **AppendEntries**: RPC to replicate log entries and act as heartbeat.
- **RequestVote**: RPC to get votes during elections.
- **CommitIndex**: highest log index known to be committed.

## Practical example (pseudo-Go) — AppendEntries handler

This shows the key checks: term, log consistency (prevIndex/prevTerm), append new entries, update commit index.

```go
func (n *Node) AppendEntries(args AppendArgs) AppendReply {
  if args.Term < n.currentTerm {
    return AppendReply{Term: n.currentTerm, Success: false}
  }
  // Reset election timeout — leader is alive
  n.resetElectionTimer()

  // Check log consistency
  if !n.matchLog(args.PrevLogIndex, args.PrevLogTerm) {
    return AppendReply{Term: n.currentTerm, Success: false}
  }

  // Append new entries (overwrite conflicts)
  n.appendEntriesFrom(args.PrevLogIndex+1, args.Entries)

  // Update commit index
  if args.LeaderCommit > n.commitIndex {
    n.commitIndex = min(args.LeaderCommit, n.lastIndex())
    n.applyEntriesUpTo(n.commitIndex)
  }

  return AppendReply{Term: n.currentTerm, Success: true}
}
```

## When to use Raft

- You need a **replicated, strongly-consistent state machine** (e.g., metadata store, leader election, configuration service).
- Your system must tolerate node crashes but requires *linearizability* for client operations.
- You prefer a protocol that's easier to reason about and implement than Paxos.

## Common pitfalls & operational tips

- **Election timeout tuning**: Too short → split votes and flapping leaders; too long → slow failover. Pick timeouts with randomness and multiples of expected heartbeat RTT.
- **Follower slowness**: Slow followers can lag and cause leader to take longer to commit or drop out of quorum. Monitor replication lag.
- **Snapshotting & compaction**: Without snapshots logs grow unbounded. Implement snapshot/install logic correctly — it's tricky to get right.
- **Changing cluster membership**: Do *joint consensus* (two-phase membership change) as described in Raft to avoid losing quorum safety.
- **Split brain & partitions**: Raft favors safety — in minority partitions, progress stops. Design for availability trade-offs where needed.
- **Premature optimization**: Start with a small, well-tested Raft library (etcd/raft, hashicorp/raft) before reimplementing.

## Diagnostics you should expose

- Current term, role (leader/follower/candidate)
- CommitIndex and LastApplied
- Per-peer matchIndex/nextIndex (replication progress)
- Election timeouts, number of elections, leader changes

---

## Before / After example (client behavior):

| Scenario | Before (naive masterless) | After (Raft leader) |
|----------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Client write during leader fail | Might succeed at some nodes and not others (inconsistency) | Fails or is retried to new leader; committed when majority persisted |
| Read after write | Client may read stale replica | If read routed via leader or uses majority read, sees latest commit |

---

## Summary

Raft provides a pragmatic, understandable way to get strong consistency across a cluster. It trades a single leader for simpler reasoning and robust guarantees: as long as a majority of nodes are alive and can talk to each other, the group makes progress without losing committed entries.

Use Raft for metadata, coordination, and any place you can't tolerate split-brain. Monitor replication metrics, tune timeouts, and implement snapshots and safe membership changes to avoid long-term issues.
keyTakeaways: Raft gives you a leader-based, majority-commit mechanism to keep replicated logs consistent and understandable.
Tune timeouts, monitor replication lag, and implement log compaction (snapshots) to keep a Raft cluster healthy.
Use battle-tested Raft libraries for production; implementing Raft yourself is a great learning exercise but easy to get wrong.
furtherReading: https://academic.oup.com/comjnl/article/48/2/213/420627 (Diego Ongaro — Raft: Consensus for Practical Systems (paper/PhD thesis))
